formulation: The rules-based international order is not in crisis — it is already dead, and the proof is that left and right have converged on identical critiques while no one at this conference proposed anything to replace it. Krastev's defense of hypocrisy as 'better than a world where only power matters' is the most sophisticated form of stagnation: preserving a fiction everyone has seen through so that no one has to build what comes next.
context_narrative: At a Munich panel titled 'The World on the Brink,' the EU's Kaja Kallas and US National Security Advisor Michael Waltz clashed over whether American pressure on the UN constitutes reform or demolition — while Saudi Arabia's Prince Faisal reminded the room that for the developing world, the brink is not new. In a separate session on the West's internal fractures, Ivan Krastev offered a striking defense: liberal hypocrisy, he argued, is better than no hypocrisy at all, because without the fiction of sovereign equality small states have no reason to distinguish the US from China. From the audience, a questioner noted that AOC's left-wing critique — 'our exceptions became our rules' — has converged with the nationalist right's, a point the panel declined to engage.

selected_quotes.quote: There is something worse than liberal hypocrisy, and that is a world with no hypocrisy at all — a world where only power matters — because then small countries have no reason to distinguish between the US and China, and American exceptionalism is reduced to being exceptionally powerful.

The critique of the rules-based order is not only coming from the right; AOC's formulation that 'we called out exceptions to our values and our rules, and eventually our exceptions became our own rules' suggests a left-wing critique that may overlap with the right's, which the panel should engage.

The panic about the breakdown of the rules-based order is a Eurocentric awakening; much of the developing world has lived with might-makes-right for over a decade, and the current conversation is finally catching up to their reality.
selected_quotes.attribution: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud

selected_quotes.quote: In a rules-based order, hypocrisy is vulnerability — the West's habit of looking the other way for inconvenient populations undermines the legitimacy of the entire system and threatens democracies globally.

Does the United States still believe in a multilateral system where rules would constrain its own actions, or only in multilateral arrangements it controls? The moderator asked this directly; Waltz answered with examples of results-oriented engagement but never affirmed that the US accepts constraints on itself.

rationale: This proposition lands in Thiel's zero-to-one and stagnation territory, directly challenges the most sophisticated defense of the status quo offered at the conference (Krastev's 'necessary hypocrisy'), and leverages the left-right convergence that his Straussian-Girardian framework is uniquely positioned to decode — forcing him to say whether the fiction is still load-bearing or whether naming it as dead is itself the zero-to-one move.
